nodes.title: Check elapsed time before running
nodes.why: This is the core throttle rule: only run when enough time has passed since the previous execution.
nodes.alternative: Use a timer-based cooldown flag instead of comparing timestamps.
nodes.tradeoff: Timestamp logic stays lightweight, but behavior depends on clock reads and exact timing boundaries.

nodes.title: Call with current context and arguments
nodes.why: Using `apply` keeps method calls correct, so `this` and arguments behave exactly like the original function.
nodes.alternative: Call `fn(...args)` directly when context does not matter.
nodes.tradeoff: Slightly more verbose, but safer for reusable helpers that may wrap object methods.

nodes.title: Record execution time after successful run
nodes.why: We update the baseline only when a call actually runs, so the next window starts from real execution time.
nodes.alternative: Track a next-allowed timestamp and compare `now` against that value.
nodes.tradeoff: This approach is easy to follow, while next-allowed timestamps can be clearer in some edge-case logic.

nodes.title: Guard calls while cooldown is active
nodes.why: When `waiting` is true, we skip extra calls. That guarantees at most one execution per interval window.
nodes.alternative: Use `Date.now()` and compare against a stored last-run timestamp.
nodes.tradeoff: Flag-based logic feels intuitive with timers, but timestamp logic can be easier to test deterministically.

nodes.title: Start cooldown immediately after execution
nodes.why: We flip the guard right after running `fn` so any burst that follows is suppressed until cooldown ends.
nodes.alternative: Schedule the lock indirectly via a separate helper function.
nodes.tradeoff: Inline state updates are explicit and fast, but helper abstractions may read cleaner in larger utilities.

nodes.title: Release cooldown using setTimeout
nodes.why: The timer defines the throttle window length and re-enables execution once the interval has passed.
nodes.alternative: Store a timeout id and allow cancellation/reset behavior.
nodes.tradeoff: Simple for leading-only throttle, but less flexible if you later need trailing execution controls.
